text: Local recurrence is one of the most important problems of rectal cancer surgery. One of the ways to reduce frequency of relapses is addition of lateral pelvic dissection to traditional TME. While TME is currently the standard of treatment for rectal cancer, lateral pelvic dissection is a debatable option and is performed in various clinical scenarios. The first is implementation of lateral pelvic dissection to virtually all patients. This approach is generally accepted in Japan, where lateral dissection is recommended for all rectal tumors located below the peritoneum of the pelvic floor or invading muscularis propria. The second approach is wide use of neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy before performing TME. which allowed many European centers to reduce local recurrence dramatically. In our center, we use chemoradiotherapy routinely for locally advanced lower rectal cancer and in cases of remaining metastatic lateral lymph nodes on MRI after the end of neoadjuvant treatment. We perform lateral lymph node dissection on the affected side simultaneously to TME.

Before we talk about the technique of lateral lymphadenectomy, let's have a look at pelvic anatomy. This is how pelvic bones look in lateral projection. The location of the greater and less sciatic notches and the obturator foramen. Now we can see pelvic ligaments, including the sacro-tubercular and sacro-spinal cord. For us, these ligaments are important because they convert greater and less sciatic notches into greater and less sciatic foramens. Inguinal ligament is located here. Let's proceed to muscular structures. Psoas major that passes through large pelvis and goes to the thigh. Piriformis muscle, starting from the sacrum and emerging through a greater sciatic foramen. The internal obturator muscle, starting from the obturator membrane and leaving pelvis through lesser sciatic foramen. One of the most important nerve structures of the pelvis is lumbosacral trunk, originating from sacral nerves. It gives the largest nerve bundle in pelvis, the sciatic nerve. An important branch of L-II-III-IV nerve plexus is the obturator nerve that emerges from under the psoas muscle and goes straight to the obturator canal. Common iliac vessels after entering pelvis divide into external and internal. External iliac artery goes to the thigh, giving lower epigastric artery to anterior abdominal wall. Internal iliac artery splits into anterior and posterior parts. Large superior gluteal artery emerges from the posterior one and makes its way through a greater sciatic foramen. There is also a lateral sacral artery. Umbilical artery originates from the anterior part of internal iliac artery. It plays an important role in fetal blood supply.

The obturator artery is seen entering the obturator canal. In females, the uterine artery is visible. The inferior gluteal and pudendal arteries exit the pelvis through the greater sciatic foramen. The ureter crosses the external iliac artery on the right side as it enters the pelvis, descending and being crossed by the uterine artery posteriorly. The bladder and uterus are visible, highlighting the area of interest for lateral pelvic dissection. A laparoscopic view shows the bladder, cervix, and uterus with appendages. The uterine artery in the paracervix separates the paravesical and parorectal spaces, with the latter known as Lutzko's space.

During the lateral pelvic lymphadenectomy in a female pelvis, the assistant applies traction to the round ligament of the uterus. The surgeon dissects the peritoneum parallel to the psoas muscle along the upper edge of the external iliac vessels. The posterior and anterior leaves of the broad ligament of the uterus are sequentially peeled off the lateral pelvic wall, revealing the umbilical artery. The obturator nerve is visualized as spaces are created near large vessels for dissection.

The surgeon expands the dissection area anteriorly, revealing the external iliac vessels bending over the pubic bone under the inguinal ligament. The anterior border of dissection is marked by the pubic bone. The dorsal border is determined by the bifurcation of the common iliac vessels, with the left ureter crossing at this point. The dissection continues to visualize the edge of the large lumbar muscle, marking the upper limit of the dissection zone. The ureter remains medial, and the uterine artery creates two spaces: the parorectal and paravesical spaces. The paravesical space is anterior to the uterine artery, with the pubic bone as its anterior wall, marked in gray.

The surgeon identifies the paravesical space, marked in green, divided by the umbilical artery into lateral and medial parts. The pararectal space is visible behind the uterine artery, located laterally to the ureter. The projection line for the creation of Okabayashi and Yobuki spaces is medial to the ureter. Splunking nerves, including the hypogastric nerve and lower hypogastric plexus, are visible in these interfacial spaces. The surgeon avoids creating these spaces to prevent nerve intersection. Lymphadenectomy begins with dissection along the edge of the large lumbar muscle to the anterior border, moving the layer of fatty tissue medially and applying traction.

The surgeon continues to separate the fatty tissue from the front angle of the paravesical space, revealing the wall of the distal part of the external iliac vessels. The fatty tissue is peeled off the pelvic wall while preserving the vessels. The surgeon identifies the cupal ligament as the landmark for the front dissection boundary and separates the remaining fatty tissue from the ventral border, exposing the adventitial shells of the external iliac vessels. Dissection proceeds caudally, peeling off fatty tissue from the large lumbar muscle, with the lower border marked by the lower edge of the obturator nerve and the level of gluteal veins. The internal obturator muscle and the lateral wall of the paravesical space are visualized, along with the obturator nerve, which is opened by cutting the fiber along its edge. This facilitates lymphadenectomy, starting with the removal of fatty tissue from the external group of lymph nodes, exposing the iliac vessels, followed by the internal group. The obturator group has been mobilized, and the projection of the obturator neurovascular bundle is intact. The lymph nodes are removed, completing the dissection, with the final view showing the surgical field.
